title: Slab-built wall-mounted coat hook
subtitle: Flat oval back plate with a projecting clay hook arm for light items
excerpt: A wall hook with a flat oval or rectangular slab back plate and a projecting hook arm built from a thick coil or slab strip. The back plate has countersunk screw holes for wall fixing. Air-dry clay is not load-bearing, so this hook suits light items such as keys or thin scarves. No kiln is required.
sourceNotes: Technique derived from first principles. Encyclopedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) pottery entry consulted for historical context on hand-building methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness; the back plate of this hook is a slab, and the hook arm may also be cut from a slab strip
A rolled rope of clay; a thick coil, bent and flattened, forms the hook arm in this project
The technique of scratching cross-hatch marks into both clay surfaces and applying slip before pressing them together, critical for bonding the hook arm to the back plate
A creamy mixture of clay and water used as adhesive at the hook-arm join; the joint fails if slip is not applied
The drying stage at which clay holds its shape firmly, allowing the hook arm to be bent to its final angle before being attached
The drying stage at which all moisture has left the clay body and the piece is ready for painting and sealing
Fully dried but unfired clay that is fragile and must not be wetted before sealing
A tapered recess around a screw hole so that the screw head sits flush with the surface rather than projecting above it; made here by widening the hole mouth with the needle tool

content.text: This wall hook has a flat oval
slab
back plate and a projecting hook arm made from a thick
coil
bent at approximately 45 degrees and joined to the back plate using
score-and-slip

. No kiln is required. Air-dry clay is not load-bearing: this hook is suitable for light items only, such as keys, a small bag, or a thin scarf. Do not hang coats, umbrellas, or anything exceeding approximately 200 g.

materials.name: Two small screws (for wall fixing, 20-25 mm)

content.text: Preparation
Prepare
slip

by mixing a small amount of clay with water to a thick cream consistency. The back plate and the hook arm are built in the same session and joined before the clay has had time to stiffen. The screw holes must be formed while the clay is still soft; they cannot be drilled cleanly after the clay is bone-dry without risking cracking. Knead all clay for two minutes before starting.
Construction

Make the back plate. Roll a slab 10 mm thick. Cut an oval approximately 120 mm tall by 80 mm wide, or a rectangle 120 mm by 80 mm if a rectangular profile is preferred. The extra thickness (10 mm rather than the standard 5-7 mm) provides the mass needed to accept the screw holes and support the hook arm join.

Pierce the screw holes. Using the needle tool, pierce two holes through the back plate: one 20 mm from the top edge and centred horizontally, the second 20 mm from the bottom edge and centred horizontally. Push the needle tool through cleanly and widen each hole to at least 5 mm diameter. Create a
countersink

recess on the front face of each hole by pressing the tapered end of the needle tool handle (or a short blunt pencil) into the hole opening to widen the mouth into a taper so screw heads will sit flush.

Add surface decoration to the front of the back plate while the clay is still soft. Press a stamp or incise a design across the upper two-thirds of the front face, avoiding the area where the hook arm will attach (the lower centre area).

Make the hook arm. Roll a thick coil approximately 18 mm in diameter and 90 mm long. Flatten it slightly by pressing with the wooden rib to create a D-shaped cross section (rounded on top, flat on the underside). This shape gives more surface area at the wall join. Bend the coil at a point 25 mm from one end to create an upward angle of approximately 45 degrees. The short section (25 mm) is the wall contact end; the longer section (65 mm) projects outward as the hook.

Join the hook arm to the back plate. Identify the attachment point on the front of the back plate: the lower centre, positioned so the projecting hook will sit at the correct height. Score the contact surface of the hook arm's short end and the corresponding area on the back plate front face. Apply slip to both surfaces and press firmly together. The hook arm should project forward at 45 degrees and slightly upward so items will not slide off. Hold the join for 60 seconds.

Reinforce the join. Apply a small fillet of soft clay around the base of the hook arm where it meets the back plate, smoothing it in with a fingertip or wooden rib. This fillet distributes any load across a wider area of the join. Smooth all surfaces with a damp sponge.
Allow the assembled hook to reach the
leather-hard
stage before handling. Support the projecting hook arm with a small wad of scrunched paper beneath it so it does not sag downward under gravity while the join is still wet.
Drying
Lay the hook face-down on the board with the hook arm supported by the scrunched-paper prop. Allow 48-3 days at room temperature to reach the
bone-dry
stage. The
greenware
hook arm is fragile at the join before sealing. Do not apply downward pressure to the hook arm during drying.
Finishing

Paint with acrylic paint and allow to dry for two hours. Apply two coats of PVA diluted 50:50 with water to all surfaces including around the screw holes. PVA sealing consolidates the join as well as protecting the surface. To mount the hook on the wall, hold it against the wall and mark through the screw holes with a pencil. Drill wall fixings and drive screws so the heads sit flush in the countersink recesses.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof and is not structurally load-bearing. This hook is suitable for light items only: keys, thin scarves, lightweight bags. Do not hang coats or any item exceeding 200 g. Keep indoors away from humidity. Wipe with a dry cloth. Do not submerge in water.
Variations
Multi-hook bar: make a rectangular back plate 300 mm wide and attach three hook arms at equal spacing along its length for a small key rack.
Leaf-shaped back plate: cut the back plate in a large leaf outline rather than an oval for a more decorative silhouette.
Incised name plate: incise a name or initial into the back plate above the hook arm so the piece doubles as a personalised key hook.

rows.problem: Hook arm droops downward during drying
rows.cause: The join area was too wet and the weight of the arm caused it to sag before the clay stiffened
rows.fix: As soon as sagging is noticed, support the arm with a paper prop beneath it to hold the 45-degree angle. At leather-hard, the arm should be firm enough to hold its shape

rows.cause: The hole was pierced too small initially
rows.fix: At leather-hard, re-pierce the hole with the needle tool and widen to 5 mm. At bone-dry, drill through carefully with a hand drill using a 5 mm drill bit

rows.problem: Hook arm separates from back plate
rows.cause: Score-and-slip was not applied to both surfaces, or the fillet reinforcement was insufficient
rows.fix: Re-score both surfaces at leather-hard, apply fresh slip, press together, and build up the fillet reinforcement on all sides of the join. At bone-dry, use epoxy adhesive as an alternative fix

rows.problem: Back plate cracks through the centre
rows.cause: The plate was too thin for its size, or dried too rapidly
rows.fix: Fill the crack at leather-hard with fresh slip and smooth. Increase thickness to 10 mm on any remake. Dry slowly away from draughts
